data.title: Kimberly-Clark outlines 70-80 bps margin expansion plan as it cites $150M-$170M potential input cost inflation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kimberly-Clark (KMB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Our first quarter results underscore the strong progress we're making" as "innovation helped fuel our delivery of solid organic sales growth with volume plus mix growth increasing to 3%." (Chairman & CEO Michael Hsu)
  • "We're building market share across our key focus areas of Baby Care, Women's Health and Active Aging" and described "a second quarter launch slate that's 1 of our most active ever" while adding, "We look forward to seamlessly plugging Kenvue brands and businesses into our proven durable operating model." (Chairman & CEO Hsu)
  • "For the second quarter... we're going to be facing around a $20 million top line impact from the California DC fire" and "in the bottom line, we expect to have in the second quarter around $50 million" from inflation tied to "the Middle East war" and fire impacts; for the back half, "if we assume that oil prices remain at around $100 per barrel on average, we would be facing potentially gross incremental input costs of around $150 million to $170 million." (Senior VP & CFO Nelson Urdaneta)
  • "Our overall weighted average promo intensity in North America is down versus pre-COVID versus category levels" and "we are using more promotion to drive trial" in diapers, adding, "We've seen household penetration and velocities up on that post promotion." (President & COO Russell Torres)

Outlook

  • "We do expect organic sales growth to be slightly below Q1" in Q2, with "the strongest comp versus 2025" and "a little bit of a headwind from the distribution center fire in California," while "as we go into the second half, we expect organic growth to actually accelerate." (CFO Urdaneta)
  • "For the full year, we expect gross margin, operating profit margin to expand both in the vicinity of 70 to 80 basis points" and "we expect for the balance of the year to continue to see expansion in operating profit margins." (CFO Urdaneta)
  • Management said it has not embedded the potential back-half oil-driven inflation into its outlook yet: "We've not built this into the outlook" and "we have also not built in any potential mitigations." (CFO Urdaneta)

Financial Results

  • "In the first quarter... solid organic sales growth" was paired with "volume plus mix growth increasing to 3%" and the CEO framed it as "ninth or tenth quarter of solid volume/mix growth." (Chairman & CEO Hsu)
  • "Gross margin versus the prior year was slightly down 60 basis points" while "we expanded operating profit margins again this quarter by about 20 basis points" and "overheads of 13%, 90 basis points lower than the prior year." (CFO Urdaneta)
  • Productivity remained central to funding investment and buffering shocks: "We've delivered 2 years of 6% gross productivity back to back" and "this first quarter of the year, we're already at 6%" with "plans... to deliver for the full year 6%." (CFO Urdaneta)

Q&A

  • Dara Mohsenian, Morgan Stanley: "If oil stays what's now above $100 a barrel... walk us through the range of potential actions" and North America pricing; Chairman & CEO Hsu said, "We're not renting that through promotion" and emphasized "PNOC... pricing net of commodity input cost discipline," while CFO Urdaneta detailed the "$150 million to $170 million" potential cost and said, "we have several levers" including "revenue growth management" and supplier/hedging tools.
  • Peter Grom, UBS: "updated your outlook for category growth to 2.5% versus 2% previously"; Chairman & CEO Hsu said "North America categories rebounded strongly" and "our outlook... is like 2.5% across our categories globally," while President & COO Torres added, "we aren't really seeing any large-scale shifts in consumer buying behavior."
  • Javier Escalante Manzo, Evercore ISI: asked how the "new organizational structure" helps "restore growth at Kenvue" and merger approvals; Chairman & CEO Hsu said Kenvue challenges were "largely executional" and cited pressure points including "North America skin care, North America oral care... and... China," adding "we're confident that Kenvue will improve this year" and "the talent... from both sides is about 50-50."
  • Lauren Lieberman, Barclays: asked about shipments vs. consumption and Q2 mitigation; CFO Urdaneta said "consumption was ahead of shipments by around 200 basis points" and Q2 includes "$20 million" fire headwind; on mitigation, Chairman & CEO Hsu said, "we don't really feel like we know" full-year costs yet, while CFO Urdaneta said the company is "about 80% covered" and is "renegotiating" supplier items and reviewing "price pack architecture."
  • Anna Lizzul, BofA: asked about pacing and margins; CFO Urdaneta said margins "actually pick up as the year progresses" and reiterated full-year expansion "in the vicinity of 70 to 80 basis points."
  • Robert Moskow, TD Cowen: questioned resilience given prior tariff year; Chairman & CEO Hsu said 2022-2023 saw "$1.6 billion" and "$1.7 billion" cost years and pointed to share momentum: "we were up in 95% of sales weighted markets on share in North America and 84% in international."
  • Edward Lewis, Rothschild & Co Redburn: asked about tier performance and shipping disruptions; President & COO Torres said "the premium side... remains healthy" and "we haven't yet seen a significant impact" from strait disruptions; Chairman & CEO Hsu highlighted "Vietnam business was strong double-digit growth" and "births were up 6.5%" in Korea with "the category... up 20%" where K-C has "over a 60% share."
  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo: asked what’s different in commodity management and whether pricing could hurt volumes; Chairman & CEO Hsu said, "everything has changed about commodity management" and CFO Urdaneta said the company built "muscle around risk management" and "strategic relationships with suppliers" plus an "integrated margin management approach."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to pressing, focused on unmodeled costs and credibility of offsets, including "how nervous to be" (Robert Moskow, TD Cowen) and repeated probing on mitigation timing from Barclays and BofA.
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded in Q&A on costs, including "we're kind of keeping the cards a little close to the vest" (Chairman & CEO Hsu) while reiterating confidence through "pricing net of costs" discipline (CFO Urdaneta).
  • Versus last quarter, current-quarter tone introduced more near-term disruption detail (fire/war/oil) and more emphasis on scenario uncertainty, while last quarter language leaned on "robust, achievable plan" and steadier category assumptions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Category outlook language shifted from "around 2% globally" for 2026 (Chairman & CEO Hsu, Q4 2025) to "2.5% across our categories globally" (Chairman & CEO Hsu, Q1 2026), with management attributing Q1 strength to a North America rebound.
  • New near-term operational and cost items dominated Q1: "$20 million" Q2 sales impact from the "California DC fire" and potential "$150 million to $170 million" back-half costs if oil averages "$100 per barrel" (CFO Urdaneta). In Q4, the prominent discrete item was a "60 basis points" full-year headwind from club-channel distribution loss (CFO Urdaneta).
  • Management’s North America promo messaging remained consistent: Q1 repeated "we're not renting that through promotion" (Chairman & CEO Hsu) and Q4 similarly said it was "not interested in renting share through promotion" (Chairman & CEO Hsu).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Middle East war"-driven inflation and energy-linked inputs were highlighted as variables, with CFO Urdaneta saying costs are "moving by the day" and the company has not yet embedded the full back-half scenario into outlook.
  • Operational disruption risk was explicit: "around a $20 million top line impact from the California DC fire" plus near-term profit headwinds, with management expecting to "recover that in the second half of the year." (CFO Urdaneta)
  • Management positioned mitigation around coverage and tools: "about 80% covered" via contracts/hedging, plus "revenue growth management" and supplier renegotiations. (CFO Urdaneta)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 momentum as innovation-led, with "volume plus mix growth increasing to 3%" and continued market share gains, while framing Q2 as a transition quarter pressured by a "$20 million" distribution-center fire impact and a "$50 million" profit headwind. The company kept its full-year margin-expansion framework of "70 to 80 basis points" but repeatedly emphasized uncertainty around oil-driven inflation, citing a potential "$150 million to $170 million" back-half input-cost scenario at "$100 per barrel" and pointing to PNOC discipline, productivity, and revenue growth management as the core offset levers.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Bank targets $200M 2026 loan growth as it works through small business credit costs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Bank (FRBA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Patrick Ryan said, "Earnings came in below our expectations in the first quarter. Elevated credit costs in the credit scored small business portfolio were the primary driver," adding that "all known issues in the portfolio have either been charged off in full or specific reserves have been established." He also highlighted payoff-driven balance-sheet pressure, noting "strong loan growth so far in April and healthy pipelines provide reason for optimism that we can still achieve our loan growth goals."

  • CEO Ryan described several moving pieces affecting near-term profitability, including that "The net interest margin was down slightly in the first quarter" due to "reduced purchase accounting accretion income" and "heightened deposit competition," while adding that "our strong capital levels provide significant dry powder for share buybacks should attractive buying opportunities emerge."

  • CFO Andrew Hibshman said, "For the 3 months ended March 31, 2026, we recorded net income of $7.6 million or $0.30 per diluted share," and added, "Net charge-offs increased to $5 million for the first quarter from $1.7 million in the linked quarter, almost exclusively related to our small business portfolio."

  • Chief Lending Officer Peter Cahill attributed the quarter’s slower loan growth to payoffs despite solid production, saying, "Loans closed and funded in Q1 totaled $106 million" while "Payoffs in Q1 were $73 million," and added that the quarter-end pipeline was "probable fundings of $383 million, up 15% from where it was at year-end."

Outlook

  • CFO Hibshman framed margin expectations around multiple offsets, saying, "We expect these factors in aggregate to support a relatively stable margin with the potential for some pressure should the current flat yield curve environment persist," citing expected declines in "acquisition accounting accretion" and "enhanced deposit pricing pressure" alongside higher-yielding loan replacement.

  • Chief Retail Banking Officer Darleen Gillespie said funding priorities remain centered on deposit growth and cost control, noting, "Going forward, we may see some moderation in this benefit as heightened industry competition continues to place pressure on deposit pricing," and adding, "Looking ahead in 2026, deposit growth continues to be a priority in order to fund our expected loan growth in a profitable manner and to maintain a strong net interest margin."

  • Chief Lending Officer Cahill reiterated a specific growth target, stating, "we remain confident of our plan to grow the loan portfolio by $200 million this year."

Financial Results

  • CFO Hibshman reported profitability and balance-sheet drivers, including net income of $7.6 million and that net interest income "decreased $2.2 million compared to the fourth quarter" on "lower average loan balances" and a "5 basis point decline in the net interest margin," while also stating, "At 3.69%, we believe our first quarter net interest margin remained very strong and compares favorably to our peers."

  • CFO Hibshman detailed expense and efficiency items, saying, "Noninterest expenses were $20.9 million for the first quarter compared to $17.1 million in Q4" and attributing the change partly to "a $1.9 million gain on sale of an OREO asset" booked in Q4 as a contra expense; he added, "The first quarter marked our 27th consecutive quarter of operating with an efficiency ratio below 60%."

  • CFO Hibshman provided capital and shareholder-return context, stating, "We executed a modest amount of share repurchases during the quarter," and that even with full authorization, "we could fully execute our approved $20 million buyback program and still maintain strong capital ratios."

Q&A

  • Justin Crowley, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: Asked what is driving weakness in the small business portfolio and why management believes additional negative impact can be contained; President and CEO Ryan answered, "there's no one factor," citing small-business economic stress and that "some of it, we believe was tied to folks being a little more aggressive than we would have liked on the overall marketing of the product," adding the portfolio "is down under $100 million" and management is moving quickly "either through charge-off or specific reserve."

  • Justin Crowley, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: Asked whether stress is outside SBA; President and CEO Ryan replied, "Correct. These are smaller ticket... loans that are not necessarily SBA related."

  • Justin Crowley, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: Asked about reserves and charge-offs in that book; President and CEO Ryan referenced that the quarter’s $5 million charge-offs were "almost exclusively related to that portfolio," while CFO Hibshman added, "we have about $2 million of specific reserves allocated to known problems."

  • Justin Crowley, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: Asked for detail behind stable-margin commentary and floating-rate exposure; CFO Hibshman said purchase accounting accretion was "$1.2 million" in Q1 and should decline more gradually, and added floating-rate exposure is "about 25% of the portfolio" with repricing "either right away or like the next month."

  • Justin Crowley, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: Asked if Q1 expenses are a run-rate and about AI investment; CFO Hibshman said, "the run rate in Q1 is probably pretty close to where we'd see things going forward," while President and CEO Ryan said the bank will make "strategic incremental investments" but "it's not something that we expect would be huge additional dollars."

  • David Bishop, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division: Asked about buyback aggressiveness and a nonperforming assisted living credit; President and CEO Ryan said, "we have a buyback -- approved buyback in place" and on the credit noted the bank is a participant and "we have every reason to believe we're going to be fine there," though timing depends on restructuring.

  • Jacob Civiello, D.A. Davidson & Co., Research Division: Asked whether compensation reflects hiring pressure and whether April loan growth matches mix; President and CEO Ryan said Q1 comp was driven by seasonal items, and Chief Lending Officer Cahill said the post-3/31 growth "fits right in" with mix, with some "chunkier loans" in C&I/owner-occupied.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to skeptical around credit and funding, pressing for containment, reserves, and deposit pricing detail, including questions on "what's been driving the weakness" (Justin Crowley, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division) and the "marginal cost of deposits" (David Bishop, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks but more measured in Q&A, pairing reassurance with uncertainty language such as President and CEO Ryan’s "time will tell" on small business performance, while emphasizing buffers including "strong allowance for credit losses" and "strong capital levels."

  • Versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on strong profitability and margin expansion, the current call’s tone shifted toward remediation and stabilization, led by President and CEO Ryan stating results were "disappointing" and driven by isolated small business credit costs.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The key shift was from Q4 2025 framing of "overall results were solid" (President and CEO Patrick Ryan) to Q1 2026 acknowledging underperformance and isolating the driver: "Elevated credit costs in the credit scored small business portfolio were the primary driver" (President and CEO Ryan).

  • Loan-payoff commentary remained central, but the narrative moved from Q4 describing historically high payoffs to Q1 emphasizing normalization and early-quarter rebound, with Chief Lending Officer Cahill reporting the pipeline increased to $383 million and management pointing to mid-April loan growth progress.

  • Deposit strategy language evolved from Q4’s deliberate runoff of higher-cost balances (enabled by lower funding needs) to Q1’s focus on funding loan growth amid "heightened deposit pricing pressure" (CFO Andrew Hibshman) and continued competitive promotional pricing (Chief Retail Banking Officer Darleen Gillespie).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted concentrated credit risk in the credit-scored small business portfolio, with President and CEO Ryan citing the need to "charge-off or specific reserve" problem credits and CFO Hibshman reporting charge-offs were "almost exclusively related" to that segment.

  • Management flagged margin sensitivity to funding competition and curve dynamics, with CFO Hibshman warning of "potential for some pressure" if a flat yield curve persists and if deposit pricing must rise to support growth.

  • Management acknowledged a specific nonperforming commercial real estate exposure: Chief Lending Officer Cahill said the increase in nonperforming loans was tied to "a well-secured single-borrower commercial real estate credit totaling $9.5 million," while President and CEO Ryan said timing of resolution depends on a restructuring process.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as an earnings stumble driven by elevated, largely isolated small-business credit costs and payoff-driven balance-sheet softness, while pointing to early Q2 loan growth momentum, an expanding funding pipeline, and capital flexibility (including the $20 million buyback authorization) as key supports for pursuing the stated $200 million 2026 loan growth plan amid ongoing deposit pricing competition and moderating purchase accounting accretion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Zealand says GLP-1 developed with Boehringer led to 17% weight loss in late-stage trial
data.description:
Zealand Pharma (ZLDPF) announced on Tuesday that its GLP-1 candidate, survodutide, developed with German drugmaker Boehringer Ingelheim, met the primary goals in a late-stage trial, generating up to nearly 17% weight loss.

Citing topline data from a Phase 3 trial called SYNCHRONIZE-1, the company stated that the glucagon/GLP-1 dual agonist met the co-primary endpoints in the study for adults with obesity or overweight, without type 2 diabetes.

The analysis indicated a statistically significant average weight loss of up to 16.6% over 76 weeks compared to 3.2% of weight loss seen in the placebo arm, the Danish drugmaker announced. The results were based on an efficacy estimand, which excludes patients who did not conform to the trial’s protocol.

The weekly injection also reached the other co-primary endpoint of SYNCHRONIZE-1, with up to 85.1% of those on survodutide achieving at least a 5% reduction in body weight after 76 weeks compared to 38.8% in the placebo arm.

The analysis on the achievement of primary endpoints was based on the efficacy estimand and the treatment regimen estimand, the gold standard for assessing controlled trials.

Regarding tolerability, the company stated that there had been gastrointestinal events consistent with safety results expected from the GLP-1 drug class. While the events were mild to moderate in severity and temporary, more trial discontinuations were reported during the dose escalation phase.

data.title: Meta Q1 2026 Earnings Preview: All eyes on capex allocation
data.description:
Meta (META) is scheduled to announce Q1 earnings results on Wednesday, April 29th, after market close.

Investors will be laser-focused on Meta’s capital expenditure outlook, particularly in AI, with the company planning $60B-$65B in infrastructure investments this year.

Meta closed out last year beating Wall Street expectations for revenues and profitability and setting strong expectations for first-quarter revenues, but raised the bar on expected infrastructure spending.

"We had strong business performance in 2025," said founder/CEO Mark Zuckerberg in his initial reaction to the Q4 report. "I'm looking forward to advancing personal superintelligence for people around the world in 2026."

SA contributor Mott Capital Management noted that Meta’s earnings growth is muted and “options positioning and technicals suggest downside risk post-earnings.”

Deep Value Investing, however, suggests that Meta has “shown the highest upside from AI in non-cloud revenue growth.”

“I wouldn't be surprised to see another META beat and raise in Q2. That said, I don't want to see an uptick in the FY26 CapEx guidance,” according to the analysis by the SA contributor.

The consensus EPS Estimate is $6.82 and the consensus revenue estimate is $55.56B (+31.3% Y/Y).

Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 5 upward revisions and 1 downward. Revenue estimates have seen 33 upward revisions and 0 downward.

data.title: VICI Q1 preview: Tenant strength, Las Vegas trends in focus
data.description:
VICI Properties (VICI) is set to announce its first-quarter earnings results on Wednesday, April 29, after market close.

Wall Street expects the experiential real estate investment trust to post EPS of $0.71 on revenue of $1.02B for the quarter.

During its Q4 earnings call, CFO David Kieske projected that FY 2026 AFFO is expected to be between $2.59B and $2.625B, or between $2.42 and $2.45 per diluted common share.

The company also acknowledged that the Las Vegas Strip had a "relatively softer 2025," but described it as "more of a normalization than a pullback."

Earlier this month, VICI Properties (VICI) deepened its Las Vegas footprint even further after the Golden Entertainment (GDEN) go-private transaction was approved by shareholders. The deal will add seven more Nevada casino properties to VICI's (VICI) real estate portfolio upon closing, expected sometime during the current quarter.

Seeking Alpha analyst Juxtaposed Ideas said that macro pessimism, a Las Vegas overhang, and tenant concentration have contributed to VICI's steep selloff, albeit triggering my upgraded Strong Buy rating.

The analyst highlighted that the tenants' profitable metrics, 100% rent collection, triple net lease, and inflation-protected escalators support the REIT's resilient operations.

Additionally, “VICI's diversification into experiential assets further enhances its long-term prospects while offering a speculative upside potential, assuming a valuation re-rating nearer to its diversified REIT peers,” the analyst concluded.

VICI, with a market capitalization of ~$30.38B, secures a Buy rating from the Wall Street community, Seeking Alpha authors, and the Quant Rating system.

Seeking Alpha analyst Thomas Shields rated VICI a Buy, saying that VICI's portfolio has expanded from 19 to 93 properties since 2017, with resilient dividend growth and robust free cash flow margins despite sector headwinds.

However, he warned that key risks include inflation-driven declines in discretionary spending and concentrated exposure to Caesars. But he believes that VICI's fundamentals and dividend history support its investment case.

VICI’s shares have gained 1.1% year-to-date, underperforming the benchmark S&P 500, which has risen 4.8% in the same period.

data.title: Central Bancompany anticipates $1.8B of loans repricing in 2026 as it reinvests cash at about 4.30% yield
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Central Bancompany (CBC) Q1 2026

Management view

  • John Ross (President, CEO & Director) said, "Central Bank posted net income of $111.1 million or $0.46 per fully diluted share," adding, "Return on average assets of 2.2%, NIM on an FTE basis of $4.36 percent and efficiency ratio on an FTE basis of 45.7%."
  • Ross highlighted balance-sheet momentum and credit, saying, "We remain encouraged by the continued resumption of growth in our balance sheet with ending loans excluding other consumer of nearly 6% annualized quarter-over-quarter and average deposits up 5% year-over-year," and, "Our asset quality remained consistent with 10 basis points of net charge-offs again this quarter and allowance covered 130 basis points of total loans."
  • Ross emphasized capital return, stating, "capital levels at the holding company remained well above target with approximately $1.9 billion of excess or $7.80 per share," and, "We leaned into capital deployment this quarter by announcing a meaningful increase to our quarterly dividend and repurchasing $32 million worth of our shares."
  • James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO) framed margin drivers as more deposit-mix and reinvestment than loan-yield pressure, saying, "our deposit costs came down 5 basis points if you factor out the shift higher in public funds that we kind of signaled on the last call" and "we stepped up the pace of our buying activity in March, and that continued into April as well. In fact, in April, we're seeing -- we're reinvesting that cash into about a 4.30% yield right now."

Outlook

  • James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO) did not provide formal earnings or revenue guidance, saying earlier in the company’s public-company life cycle, "we're also not going to provide forward-looking guidance" (as reiterated on the prior call) while focusing this quarter on rate drivers and balance-sheet actions.
  • On loan repricing and spreads, Ciroli said, "we repriced about $400 million in the quarter, and we anticipate about $1.8 billion more for the rest of the pricing when those loans are repricing," and added, "we continue to see loan opportunities at 300 basis points over similar maturity treasuries."
  • On deposit costs and seasonality, Ciroli said, "we anticipate that the public funds... start to come down," and added, "We still think a low 20s beta is appropriate. But because of that nature of the non-maturity deposits, that's going to take a little while to come in."

Financial results

  • EPS was $0.46 vs. $0.43 (analysts’ estimate).
  • John Ross (President, CEO & Director) said year-over-year earnings growth was driven alongside stable credit metrics: "Relative to the first quarter of 2025, net income increased $16.3 million or 17%."
  • James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO) described loan-yield resilience and fee noise, saying, "as you look at the loan yields, yes, sure, they came down 3 basis points, almost all about was loan fees," and tied the move to prepayments: "we had fewer prepayments this quarter."
  • On expense trajectory and one-time items, Ciroli said, "we've signaled that we're going to have some additional costs of around $5 million a year in terms of public company expenses," and added, "during the quarter, we owned capitalized $700,000 of the dollars that we spent."

Q&A

  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked about loan yields and rate scenarios; James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO): responded, "we repriced about $400 million in the quarter, and we anticipate about $1.8 billion more" and said that repricing "could provide some upside to where we were in NIM."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked about delinquencies edging up; Eric Hallgren (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer): said it was "concentrated to a small number of markets" and "largely attributable to a handful of commercial clients," adding, "we view it as isolated pockets of stress and not indication of systemic weakness."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked about excess liquidity and earning assets; James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO): said, "we stepped up the pace of our buying activity in March, and that continued into April" and emphasized investment constraints: "We don't like taking on a lot of convexity risk."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked about M&A prospects; John Ross (President, CEO & Director): said, "we have no real updates for you at this stage," while adding, "We are in active discussions, nothing is imminent."
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: asked about payments revenue ramp; James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO): said, "we continue to feel pretty sanguine about that business" and pointed to activity, "the consumers still spending."
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: asked about deposit competition; James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO): said, "we're not really out there competing for the yield-seeking funds," adding, "we're out there competing on service."
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: asked about buybacks and capital allocation; James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO): said, "even with the $32 million that we bought back this quarter and we stepped up the dividend we still continue to grow our excess capital number," adding, "we think the stock is still cheap."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: asked about expense sustainability and operating leverage; James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO): said, "I think the first quarter NIE is fairly loaded" and "that's a fairly sustainable run rate."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: asked about strategies to “lock in” margins and NII progression; James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO): said, "one of our biggest opportunities is to continue to invest our excess cash" and emphasized deposit work: "our market CEOs have to go out there every day and try to manually work that count with their depositors."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly pressing, centering on rate sensitivity, deposit competition, capital deployment/M&A timing, and expense sustainability (e.g., Christopher McGratty, KBW: "Can you speak to sustainability and maybe broader operating leverage expectations?").
  • Management tone was slightly positive and process-oriented, stressing stability and discipline (e.g., Eric Hallgren (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer): "we view it as isolated pockets of stress"; John Ross (President, CEO & Director): "We are in active discussions, nothing is imminent.").
  • Compared with last quarter’s inaugural call, management maintained a similar posture on M&A disclosure (Ross this quarter: "no real updates" vs. last quarter: "we will likely have no specific update... until we're actually announcing a deal").

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Profitability metrics shifted modestly: Q1 net income was $111.1 million vs. $107.6 million in Q4, while NIM was 4.36% vs. 4.41% and efficiency ratio was 45.7% vs. 47%.
  • Capital deployment became more explicit in Q1: John Ross (President, CEO & Director) cited "repurchasing $32 million worth of our shares" and "a meaningful increase to our quarterly dividend," while Q4 emphasized "the critical objective of prudently deploying our ample excess capital" without reporting a buyback.
  • The rate/deposit narrative evolved from Q4 seasonality setup to Q1 mix normalization: James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO) pointed to public-funds seasonality as anticipated and said balances "start to come down" at quarter-end.
  • Analysts shifted from IPO/strategy framing in Q4 toward mechanics in Q1—loan repricing, deposit competition, expense run-rate, and translating excess cash into NII.

Risks and concerns

  • James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO) flagged public-funds seasonality and deposit-cost inertia, saying, "90% our deposit base is non-maturity" and deposit repricing "is not something that just mechanically comes down."
  • Eric Hallgren (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer) acknowledged localized stress, saying delinquencies were "concentrated to a small number of markets" but added, "we don't anticipate those delinquencies to grade any further."
  • James Ciroli (Executive VP & CFO) noted incremental cost pressure from being public and systems work, saying, "additional costs of around $5 million a year" and, "we are still in the middle of our core conversion."

Final takeaway

Management emphasized higher Q1 earnings, continued balance-sheet growth, and disciplined capital deployment—highlighting a larger excess-capital position and stepped-up buybacks and dividend actions—while directing investor focus to loan repricing opportunities, ongoing reinvestment of excess cash, and deposit-cost normalization as public-funds seasonality fades. Credit commentary centered on limited, market-specific commercial delinquencies that management described as isolated and expected to resolve, while analyst questions concentrated on the sustainability of margin and expenses, and on the timeline for M&A versus continued buybacks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Disney shares slip as FCC scrutiny adds to Kimmel-related headwinds
data.description:
Disney’s (DIS) TV licenses may face scrutiny by the Federal Communications Commission ((FCC)) amid allegations that the media giant engaged in race- and gender-based discrimination.

A potential license review coincides with Disney (DIS) navigating the fallout from controversial comments made by comedian Jimmy Kimmel on his late-night ABC program.

President Trump and his wife Melania have both called on Disney (DIS) to remove Kimmel as the host of his eponymous TV show for saying the First Lady “had the look of an expectant widow” during coverage of a reported assassination attempt at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner.

While the FCC investigation is unrelated to Kimmel’s remarks, it’s just another headache for the entertainment conglomerate.

According to sources cited by Semafor, FCC Chairman Brendan Carr is looking into Disney’s (DIS) diversity programs, specifically, the company’s alleged practice of “invidious” discrimination based on race and gender and hiring practices that prioritize characteristics over merit.

“If the evidence does in fact play out and shows that they were engaged in race- and gender-based discrimination, that’s a very serious issue at the FCC that could fundamentally go to their character qualifications to even hold a license,” Carr was quoted as saying earlier this month.

This is not the first time either Disney (DIS) has come under FCC scrutiny, nor Kimmel for making polarizing remarks.

Last year, the FCC opened an investigation into Disney’s (DIS) diversity, equity, and inclusion ((DEI)) practices that conflict with “reasonable interpretation of federal law.”

This investigation included Disney’s (DIS) “Reimagine Tomorrow” initiative, which set a goal of 50% of Disney characters being portrayed by underrepresented groups.

Less than 6 months later, ABC (DIS) suspended Kimmel over his remarks regarding the assassination of conservative activist and Turning Point USA founder Charlie Kirk. In the wake of Kimmel’s remarks and before the suspension, Carr called his comments “truly sick” and warned that if “these companies” didn't take action to "change conduct…there's going to be additional work for the FCC ahead."  

Kimmel’s show did not air for five days in September.

News of another FCC investigation weighed on shares and drove DIS into the red for a fourth consecutive day.

Disney (DIS) has not yet responded to Seeking Alpha’s request for comment.

data.title: UAE energy chief explains decision to exit OPEC
data.description:
The United Arab Emirates on Tuesday announced its decision to leave OPEC, marking a significant shift in global oil (USO) (BNO) politics as the Strait of Hormuz closure persists.

In an interview with CNBC, UAE Energy Minister Suhail Al Mazrouei said the decision followed a “very careful and long review” of the country’s policies and strategies. He emphasized that the UAE’s international investments and aspirations require greater flexibility than membership in the cartel allows.

“The world is living at an unprecedented time where the level of supply is not adequate to what the world needs without withdrawing from the strategic reserve,” Al Mazrouei said. “This situation would require agility, would require someone to move quickly.”

The minister stressed that the timing was chosen to minimize disruption to global markets and fellow producers. With the world currently undersupplied because of the Strait of Hormuz closure, he argued the UAE’s exit would have minimal impact on prices.

Al Mazrouei was careful to distance the decision from any political tensions with Saudi Arabia, which has led OPEC’s production cut efforts. He described the move as a “pure policy change” and expressed continued respect for OPEC members.

The UAE has long pursued a production capacity target of 5M barrels per day by 2027. Al Mazrouei noted that independence from OPEC constraints would allow the country to pursue this goal alongside its gas and petrochemical ambitions. The UAE was the third-largest producer in OPEC in February, behind Saudi Arabia and Iraq.

When asked about immediate production increases, the minister acknowledged that the Strait of Hormuz closure constrains all Gulf producers. He called on the international community to address the crisis, noting its impact on consumers worldwide.

“The consumers are living a very tough time, not having the resources,” Al Mazrouei said. “We, as a responsible producer, think that we need to be on the side of the consumer.”

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: TSLA, AAPL, AMZN, INTC
data.description:
Recent analyst actions on Seeking Alpha have highlighted shifting sentiment across the technology and consumer sectors, with Apple (AAPL) and Tesla (TSLA) seeing notable upgrades. Dividend Appreciator has moved Apple to a Buy rating, citing its resilience in the Chinese market and consistent dividend growth, while Gary Alexander has upgraded Tesla based on recovering car sales and upcoming product catalysts.

On the other hand, Intel (INTC) and Amazon (AMZN) have seen a move toward more cautious ratings. Dhierin Bechai has downgraded Intel to a Hold following a significant rally that has left the stock fully valued, while Hawkinvest suggests that investors wait for a post-earnings pullback before initiating new positions in Amazon.

Upgrades

  • Apple (AAPL): Upgrade Sell to Buy by Dividend Appreciator. The analyst believes that fears surrounding geopolitical risks and innovation in China are overblown, noting that the iPhone is still gaining market share while fundamentals remain robust.

    “Apple is a solid dividend payer with an ultra-conservative payout ratio. In an uncertain world, this name offers relative safety. Your capital appears secure in a company with a rock-solid balance sheet and strong—and getting ever stronger—underlying earnings.”

  • Tesla (TSLA): Upgrade to Buy by Gary Alexander. Despite a year-to-date decline of approximately 15%, the analyst sees a rebound opportunity driven by recovering Model 3/Y sales in Europe and high-impact catalysts like the Cybercab and Optimus.

    “Investors have sold off the stock on CEO Elon Musk's statement that 2026 will require over $25 billion in capex spending to unlock factory capacity, particularly for two major new product lines that will begin production later this year: Cybercab and Optimus. These will be major catalysts that will begin to reinforce the narrative that Tesla is beyond a mere automaker, and after the latest dip, I'm upgrading my rating on Tesla to Buy.”

Downgrades

  • Intel (INTC): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Dhierin Bechai. The stock reached its base case price target after a 77.6% surge year-to-date, leading the analyst to conclude that the shares are now fully valued with limited near-term upside.

    “Due to high PC component prices and likely overall economic uncertainty, we are seeing pressures on PC sales, and Intel expects low-double-digit declines for the market this year.”

  • Amazon.com (AMZN): Hold by Hawkinvest. While the company’s AI infrastructure and cloud partnerships are thriving with a $20B+ annual chip revenue run rate, the analyst points to overbought technicals and antitrust risks as reasons to wait for a better entry price.

    “My concern is that this could be a potentially bearish "double top" formation if the stock does not punch through current levels in a significant way soon. If earnings beat expectations and guidance is strong, this might be enough to create a breakout above current levels. But I do have concerns about guidance potentially being an issue as well as the fact that I am seeing stocks sell off even after reporting strong earnings and guidance.”

data.title: Custom Truck targets $415M-$440M 2026 adjusted EBITDA as SER rental momentum stays strong
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Custom Truck One Source (CTOS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Ryan McMonagle said “2026 is off to a great start as we delivered record first quarter revenue,” reporting “revenue of $462 million and adjusted EBITDA of $98 million,” and tying the quarter’s performance to “continued strength in our Specialty Equipment Rentals segment” as transmission and distribution demand remained strong.
  • CEO McMonagle highlighted rental intensity and fleet positioning, including “Our rental fleet averaged 81.4% utilization during the quarter” and “OEC on rent, which averaged $1.34 billion in Q1,” adding that “So far in Q2, both measures have continued to strengthen with utilization and OEC on rent currently trending above our first quarter averages.”
  • CEO McMonagle emphasized fleet scale for 2026 growth, saying, “We ended the quarter with total OEC of $1.66 billion, the highest quarter end level in our history, which will support our expectation for continued growth in SER revenues this year,” and pointed to regulatory preparedness: “CTOS is well positioned with our young rental fleet, current inventory positions and strong relationships with our chassis OEM partners to navigate the impact of the EPA's 2027 emission standards.”
  • CEO McMonagle detailed STEM momentum, noting “STEM revenue, excluding sales to our SER segment, were up 5% year-over-year,” and said margin improved due to execution: “We also saw gross margin expand in the quarter, driven by significant cost out and productivity improvements led by our production team.”
  • CEO McMonagle underscored backlog acceleration, stating, “New sales order backlog ended the first quarter at $411 million,” and added, “Our backlog has continued to grow so far in Q2.”
  • CFO Christopher Eperjesy framed the quarter as broad-based improvement: “Our first quarter 2026 results reflect stronger operating performance across the business and improved rental fundamentals, particularly in our T&D end markets.”

Outlook

  • Management affirmed consolidated revenue guidance of “$2.005 billion to $2.12 billion,” while CEO McMonagle said, “Given strong conditions in the T&D end markets, we are raising both the bottom and top ends of our adjusted EBITDA guidance and now project a range of $415 million to $440 million.”
  • CFO Eperjesy described the EBITDA guide increase as operationally driven: “we raised the EBITDA guidance really because… the rental business is outperforming… [and] the operating execution that is happening,” adding, “it really is a combination of those 2, the mix and the operating execution and not so much anything on the top line.”
  • CFO Eperjesy reiterated a 2026 capital intensity shift, stating the company expects “a net investment in our rental fleet of approximately $150 million to $170 million,” alongside “non-rental CapEx of $40 million to $50 million.”
  • CFO Eperjesy maintained cash and leverage targets: “we expect to generate more than $50 million of levered free cash flow and reduce our net leverage ratio to meaningfully below 4x by the end of fiscal 2026 while progressing towards our 3x net leverage target in 2027.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Eperjesy reported Q1 results of “total revenue was $462 million and adjusted EBITDA was $98 million,” and said SER produced “third-party revenue… $194 million” and “Segment adjusted EBITDA of $105 million,” while STEM delivered “third-party revenue… $268 million” and “STEM segment adjusted EBITDA was $33 million.”
  • CFO Eperjesy quantified SER rental KPIs and pricing, stating “utilization averaged 81.4%,” “Average OEC on rent… $1.34 billion,” and “On-rent yield in the first quarter was 38.9%,” adding, “we continue to see opportunities for rate improvement as transmission mix grows and pricing discipline holds.”
  • CFO Eperjesy said leverage improved with scale and cash focus: “With LTM adjusted EBITDA of more than $408 million and net debt of $1.65 billion, we finished Q1 with net leverage of slightly more than 4x,” and added, “Free cash flow generation and deleveraging remain key focus areas for us.”
  • CFO Eperjesy explained working capital as a 2026 lever, saying inventory was “probably closer to 7.5 months right now,” and reiterated a reduction path “to our targeted range of below 6 months.”

Q&A

  • Michael Shlisky, D.A. Davidson & Co.: asked about “Section 232 tariffs” risk across quotes/backlog; CEO McMonagle responded, “we're in a pretty good spot… having inventory on the ground puts us in a good position,” while noting “a little bit of tariff exposure on some of our bodies.”
  • Michael Shlisky, D.A. Davidson & Co.: pressed on how much the fleet can age; CEO McMonagle said peer data is limited but added, “when we put the businesses together back in 2021, the average age of the fleet was just about 4 years,” and said aging can drive “real cash generation… [but] most important… is taking care of the customer.”
  • Daniel Hultberg, Oppenheimer: asked about margin/cost actions; CEO McMonagle answered, “a lot of efforts underway… to drive productivity improvement” and “rightsize the cost structure,” especially impacting “STEM gross margin.”
  • Daniel Hultberg, Oppenheimer: asked about yield and pricing embedded in guidance; CEO McMonagle said, “It was about a 5% price increase… in December,” and tied yield to “mix” as “transmission is coming on very strong,” while CFO Eperjesy added the guide raise was “not so much anything on the top line.”
  • Justin Hauke, Baird: challenged why the EBITDA raise was $5 million after a strong Q1; CFO Eperjesy cited Q2 as “a pretty tough comp,” and said, “we're certainly being prudent… We’ll adjust it as the year goes on.”
  • Justin Hauke, Baird: asked about data center pushback affecting T&D; CEO McMonagle said, “We're still seeing strong demand,” and “the specific noise around data center doesn't seem to be impacting our customers.”
  • Naim Kaplan, Deutsche Bank: asked about quoting pipeline and supply chain shortages; CEO McMonagle said transmission demand “continue[s] to pick up” and “I don't think there is any product category… [with] an issue… with availability of equipment.”
  • Brian Brophy, Stifel: asked about bidding activity and new equipment pricing; CEO McMonagle said bidding is “robust” in transmission with “several specific projects,” while CFO Eperjesy said pricing is “more stable… [though] there certainly still is some pressure.”
  • Manish Somaiya, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about normalized STEM margins and backlog/customer mix; CFO Eperjesy cited a “15% to 18% range” and said “closer to the 16% to 17% range this year,” while CEO McMonagle said backlog growth was led by “our local and regional customers,” with strength in “utility and forestry” and less pickup in “waste… and dump truck.”
  • Tami Zakaria, JPMorgan: asked how much backlog is 2026 vs beyond; CEO McMonagle said, “The far majority of it is… for 2026 deliveries.” She also asked about deleveraging; CFO Eperjesy said, “We guided levered free cash flow north of $50 million. That would all be used to pay down debt.”
  • Abraham Landa, BofA Securities: asked for inventory months-on-hand and working capital assumptions; CFO Eperjesy said inventory is “closer to 7.5 months right now,” and explained that a $100 million inventory reduction “may get $20 million of cash” due to floor plan structure.
  • Manish Somaiya, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about bottlenecks and what drives higher free cash flow; CEO McMonagle flagged supply chain dependencies (Terex, pulling/stringing suppliers, and 6x6/4x4 chassis), and CFO Eperjesy cited “incremental EBITDA,” “lower net rental CapEx,” and “working capital unlock.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly skeptical, with multiple questions pressing on durability (data centers), conservatism in guidance increases, and execution constraints; examples included Justin Hauke’s framing that Q1 appeared “$10 million to $15 million ahead” while guidance rose “by $5 million,” and repeated probing on backlog, tariffs, and inventory.
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing strength while signaling caution on guide-setting; CFO Eperjesy said, “we're certainly being prudent,” and CEO McMonagle repeatedly emphasized positioning, including, “I think we're in a good spot overall.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s prepared tone remained confident on T&D durability, while Q1 added more discussion of cost actions, tariffs, and the mechanics of segment comparability; analysts shifted from prior-quarter questions on utilization “sweet spot” and seasonality to Q1 focus on guidance conservatism, tariffs, and cash conversion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language tightened on profitability: Q4 2025 projected adjusted EBITDA of “$410 million to $435 million,” while Q1 2026 raised it to “$415 million to $440 million,” and both calls kept consolidated revenue guidance at “$2.005 billion to $2.12 billion.”
  • Segment framing changed from ERS/TES/APS in Q4 to SER/STEM in Q1, with CFO Eperjesy emphasizing that prior periods are “not fully comparable… largely because 2025 results… do not include any margin on intersegment sales.”
  • Rental fundamentals moderated sequentially from Q4 utilization “just under 84%” and OEC on rent “just under $1.4 billion” to Q1 utilization “81.4%” and OEC on rent “$1.34 billion,” while management highlighted Q2 trending “above our first quarter averages.”
  • Analysts’ emphasis evolved from Q4’s questions on utilization sustainability and quarterly cadence to Q1’s focus on tariffs, the size of the EBITDA guidance raise, and the pace/timing of inventory-driven cash generation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged tariff exposure, with CEO McMonagle saying, “We are seeing a little bit of tariff exposure on some of our bodies because of 232,” while pointing to mitigation via “inventory on the ground.”
  • Management cited supply chain as a potential execution limiter, with CEO McMonagle pointing to transmission equipment suppliers (including “Terex”) and “all-wheel drive axles… 6x6 and 4x4 chassis” as areas to monitor.
  • Management flagged uncertainty around EPA 2027 implementation details, with CEO McMonagle referencing “some of the final rulings… around the warranty and some of the questions that are still open,” while stating CTOS is positioned through fleet age, inventory, and OEM relationships.

Final Takeaway

Management positioned Q1 as a record start supported by strong SER rental fundamentals, expanding STEM margin via “cost out and productivity improvements,” and a growing backlog weighted toward 2026 deliveries. The company kept its 2026 revenue range unchanged while raising adjusted EBITDA guidance to $415 million-$440 million, tying the increase to rental outperformance and execution. Management emphasized that lower rental fleet investment, inventory reductions toward below 6 months on hand, and EBITDA growth are expected to support more than $50 million of levered free cash flow and net leverage “meaningfully below 4x” by year-end 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
